narrative:
Short impact summary

The article "Human Health and Ocean Pollution," published in the Annals of Global Health, has garnered significant academic attention with 735 citations, reflecting its importance in understanding the intersection of human health and environmental challenges.

Full narrative

Published in 2020, the research article "Human Health and Ocean Pollution" by a group of internationally recognized authors, including Philip J. Landrigan and John J. Stegeman, highlights critical issues at the nexus of marine ecosystems and public health. This work, backed by prestigious institutions such as the University of Copenhagen and Imperial College London, has accumulated 735 citations, demonstrating its substantial impact on the academic community and its relevance to ongoing discussions about pollution and health.

The article's resonance across multiple disciplines is evident through its citation landscape. Notably, it is frequently referenced in esteemed journals such as the Marine Pollution Bulletin and The Science of The Total Environment, indicating its widespread applicability and significance in environmental health research. The work has influenced a range of scholarly outputs, including 471 articles and 104 reviews, which underscores its role as a foundational text in this critical area of study.

Institutionally, the research has received substantial support from significant funding agencies like the World Health Organization and the National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences, validating the urgency of addressing ocean pollution and its health implications. This institutional backing enhances the credibility of the findings and encourages further exploration in policy and community health strategies.

Given the increasing global focus on environmental health, the article serves as a vital resource for future research. Its proven downstream reuse, recorded at 749 instances, indicates that the methodologies and data outlined have been actively deployed in subsequent studies, continually forwarding knowledge in this domain. As awareness of the effects of ocean pollution on human health grows, this work stands out as a crucial reference for scholars, policymakers, and advocates alike.
